blocks.html: This is the last set of consonants! They're even considerably rarer than the ones in the previous chapter and appear mostly in big words, but it's still necessary to know them of course!
These consonants are SO rare that I struggled to find common Thai words that contain them!
Sometimes ฑ is pronounced [d]. However, บัณฑิต is the only such word worth knowing.

Phew! We've done ALL 42 Thai consonants! However, it is worth looking back and seeing how much you can remember about the shape, sound, and the class of each consonant. Use the mnemonics you've learnt from pages 42-43 to help you memorise consonant classes (Underlined characters are the most common character in each sound group).

When Thais borrow words from other languages, sometimes we don't (or can't) pronounce all the consonants in the words but we want to preserve the original spellings. So what we do is 'kill off' unpronounced consonants by marking them with a silent mark (-์ ) called ไม้ทัณฑฆาต /mái tuntákâat/.
First, can you guess how you should pronounce these words?
Silent marks can also kill the preceding consonant too:
It doesn't matter whether there's a vowel — kill them all!

Some long vowels have their short counterparts: e.g. -ิ [i] is the short version of -ี [ee], etc. However, most long vowels like เ- or แ- do not, so -ะ is added to the end of the syllable to create a shorter vowel sound.
This shortening -ะ symbol is ONLY used with syllables that don't have a final consonant.
Remember, however, that เ-าะ [-or'] is NOT a short version of เ-า [-ao]! They're different!
You might have noticed the tone changes in these shortened words. Remember? If a syllable ends with a short vowel, it is DEAD, thus the shortening of the vowel also affects the tone.

For เ-, แ- and -อ however, if there's a FINAL CONSONANT then this shortening mark (-็) "ไม้ไต่คู้" /mái dtàikóo/ will be used instead of -ะ. This mark goes over the initial consonant. It should be noted that the shortening mark -็ will never appear with a tone mark.

This practice deals with two kinds of words: those with a silent mark and those with a shortening mark. For the words with a silent mark, read them and circle around all the unpronounced characters (there are a few difficult ones, so prepare to be wrong!); for the words with a shortening mark, namely -ะ or -็, read them and rewrite them as the long counterpart of their vowel e.g. โกะ /gò/ >> โก /goh/. Once you change the vowel to a long vowel, keep in mind that there might be a tone change, so you have work that out too.

You know ร as a low class 1 consonant [r], but in fact it has quite a few quirks that you need to pay attention to. ร may also be a vowel, change sounds from [r] to something else, or not be pronounced at all!
It's pronounced [-n]. This one we've already learnt on DAY 1:
If it's a final consonant and there's no written vowel, it's perhaps pronounced [-orn]. We've learnt this as well on DAY 3:
ร becomes silent at the end of the syllable when it isn't part of any syllable even without the silent mark:
Sometimes it's ambiguous whether final ร should be pronounced as [-n], [-orn], or silent. So if you can, always consult a Thai person for accuracy.
ร in false consonant clusters สร, ศร, ซร (i.e. clusters with an [s] consonant and ร together) is silent:
ทร combination is almost always pronounced [s] (Unless it's in loanwords, see ATTENTION! on the following section):
บริ combination is pronounced [borrí], not [bàrí] (Unless it's in loanwords, see ATTENTION! on the following section):
Or ร is just not pronounced for no reason! Sorry, but sometimes we just want to see you suffer. Fortunately, such cases are not common:
The combination ทร and บริ are usually pronounced [tr-] and [bri-] in foreign proper nouns: เทรน /trayn/ 'train', ทริค /trík/ 'trick', บริดจ์ /brìt/ 'bridge', บริง /bring/.
Two ร's together form an -ัน [-un] sound:
But if there's a final consonant, it's just pronounced -ั- [-u-]:

This reading exercise has a twist: it is written in stylised font! You'll probably take longer than usual to identify all the letters. Be careful not to get similar letters mixed up. This type of font is ubiquitous in advertising and publishing so it's worth familiarising yourself with it. Take your time, there's no rush!

items: Mái tuntákâat (-์ ) marks the unpronounced consonant and/or vowel located directly under it, and in some cases the preceding consonant and vowel too
The length of many long vowels can be shortened by adding -ะ after the syllable (this only occurs with long vowels that have no short counterpart)
Mái dtàikóo (-็ ) is only used for shortening syllables with เ-, แ- or -อ vowel and a final consonant
ร becomes [-n] or [-orn] at the end of the syllable
ร is silent when following [s] consonants (ซ, ส, ศ, ษ); at the end of the syllable when it doesn't seem to belong there; or sometimes it just is, without any reason!
ทร combination makes an [s] sound, บริ combination makes [borri-] sound (unless in loanwords)
Double รร is pronounced [-un] when there's no final consonant, or [-u-] when there's a final consonant
